sentences.text: Stalingrad.
Yeah.
I went from [INAUDIBLE], which was a town where the army located.
And I walk 35 kilometer to Stalingrad.
And in Stalingrad, is a-- a very big fight.
And I saw, one day, [?
Khrushchev, ?] like a general, general [INAUDIBLE], very close.
Where I stand in the-- I called it the [INAUDIBLE].
You know, in the [INAUDIBLE], like this.
You know?
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one day, my officer comes over and said, I-- you, you, you, you, you five soldiers.
I'm the fifth.
I'm a lucky man.
I take that.
He said, you see, this has a big building, empty.
You have to go there and look where the German located.
And tell us.
And then the Russian [INAUDIBLE] go, [INAUDIBLE].
Said yes, ?] and I went.
You know?
He-- I come off the third floor.
And this was an empty building.
I met the other fellow, [INAUDIBLE], a gypsy, a very nice, a sweet boy.
Hour before, played me the-- the accordion.
[SIGHS] And I went with mine four sergeants inside.
And there's three Russian, one gypsy, and I.
The Germans see when-- I and mine sergeants walks in.
And I feel blood here.
And there's a little river-- not the Volga.
The Volga's a big river-- a little river to the knee.
And I feel, if I now go over there.
I'm bloody-- bloody You know?
Up here.
I went over the river.
I don't remember nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when I wake up three days later, I'm in the hospital.
A few weeks later, sent me back.
Yeah.
Sent me back.
And then I remember, in 1943, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] end of 1943, is the Russians start to push out the German.
Not from Stalingrad.
The army go around Stalingrad.
And-- And she put on, like this, the [? whole ?] army [? join. ?] And then I take a lot of Germany and the Italian soldiers to the train, and take them to the city of Volsk, and to the hospitals.
But the German soldiers don't have nothing for them.
Everything, you know, is frozen.
And from there, I went to Kijew. 1943, I take Kijew.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Kijew, in English, it's Kijew.
No?
Right.
K-I-E-V, that city?
No.
K-I-J-E-W.
And I come there.
And I was fighting with mine army there.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and there, a lot of things that was happened there, it's nothing important.
You know?
And then in 1946, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went back to Poland.
Yeah.
In 19-- end-- end of-- in the beginning from 1946, I go out in the army.
Yeah.
From Kijew.
And from Kijew, I went straight to Poland.
And I come not through Lublin.
And I come.
And that city, which the Polish army take from the German away.
But for this German, called in German, a Breslau, in Polish, Wroclaw.
And I come in 1946, in that summer.
The old city, 85% is damaged.
You don't have streets.
You walk over the houses.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I find a little room when I look to this ceiling, I see the sky.
[LAUGHS] I'm happy.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm happy, and I'm young.
I'm in the 20s.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In 1952, I get married.
I want tell you, yeah.
And this time, when I had to go back, no trains, no nothing.
Still.
Because I come as [INAUDIBLE], not-- not a passenger from the border, from the Russian border, when I go to Poland.
I come at the-- with a-- with a--
A trains, yeah.
I didn't have [INAUDIBLE].
In 1952, I get married. 1954, my son is born.
Yeah.
I lived in the city of Wroclaw.
And I went for more education.
And I went for three years, in a special institute.
And I make in Poland, a higher education.
What?
What I take there?
A lot of things.
A lot of history and a lot of the geography.
A lot of-- a lot of things, there.
You know, like in the high schools.
Because it's more like a high school.
You know?
And I make this.
In three years, I have to go five.
I make in three years because it make the faster there.
And still, I worked for my profession.
And my profession, I worked in Poland.
And this, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's called [NON-ENGLISH].
This is a [? house of ?] [? models. ?] You know, a very nice place to work.
And I went in 1951.
No.
Yeah, 1951, I went to Lublin, to look around, maybe somebody lived.
No.
I come to Lublin.
Not only the house where I lived, nor the street.
The whole neighborhood is like a one place.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I meet-- when I come back to Wroclaw, I meet my friend, what I'm born the same city, a girl.
I have a picture.
I can find it and look.
But they are all there.
A lot of [?
Jew ?] boys and girls.
And somebody, I meet somebody, in Wroclaw, a [INAUDIBLE] friend from the [INAUDIBLE], in the old city, said, you know what?
Hela ?], her name is in Polish, [?
Hela ?], that [?
Chaya ?] in Yiddish.
She lived in a small city here, not too far from here, two hours, maybe hour and a half, with the bus to go.
She's married a partisan.
And she have two children.
I went there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I find him.
I lock the door.
[INAUDIBLE], a leather coat.
I asked him, Mr.
Meinster, ?] the name, the second name, [?
Meinster. ?] He said, [INAUDIBLE] come in.
And Hela, [? see? ?] She said, yes.
No, she's with the children.
She sleep with the children.
They're little children.
She's a beautiful girl.
She has something [INAUDIBLE].
[LAUGHS] She comes out, [LAUGHS] and she start crying.
She recognized me.
[INAUDIBLE].
[LAUGHS] She looked and balding.
I even met-- ?] And when [INAUDIBLE] that, what I tell you, I lost, in three days, the hair.
And put more [INAUDIBLE].
She sit with me five hours.
Because in 1942, the German make the ghetto of Lublin.
Take every Jew in that special ghetto, in that special neighborhood.
This neighborhood called Majdan.
And this neighborhood make the second bigger concentration camp, Majdanek.
I can spell you if you want.
M-A-J-D-E-K.
[SIGHS] In there, every Jew in this neighborhood.
And she lived in the same house with my parents, with my sister, and with the children, and my brother-in-law.
She worked in a restaurant, which cooked only for the German officers.
It's a lot of doctors, a lot of [? engineers, ?] Jewish, [INAUDIBLE] saved her life, went there to work in this restaurant.
But the peoples work in this restaurant, the Germans don't kill.
She tell me there's a one officer, a German officer, was to come every day to this restaurant.
And she was our waiter.
She was a beautiful girl.
And said, Hela, when the Gestapo go and kill everybody in the ghetto, I will save your life.
She said, yeah, I'll give you good food.
You tell me like [? this ?].
And on one day, this was the 14th [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of November, in 1943.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She is now one room with her brother.
And the brother, 5 o'clock in the morning in November, [INAUDIBLE] you up.
Hela, look what's happened?
The whole ghetto's on fire.
[INAUDIBLE] Germany and the [?
Latvian ?] Police.
Latvia, ?] [?
Latvian ?].
She think if she jumped in, in this restaurant, she could save their life.
When she jumped in, everybody scared of her in there.
And she's only in the nightgown.
And it's cold.
November in Poland, it's-- it's 20 below the zero.
She didn't know what to do.
At the same time, the German officer comes in and said, jump in the-- in the cellar.
I go save your life.
This German officer.
And right [? out ?] of Germany, and here is-- [INAUDIBLE] The German looked over at it, don't look here.
But the German went with dogs, made somebody hide him in a house.
She stay in there three days.
And the German officer bring there, bread.
And one day, I come over and bring the water to wash.
I said, I come back in an hour.
And I go take you out from the ghetto.
I make you free.
She have papers, as she's a Polish girl.
She's Catholic.
You know?
She-- she make, before, papers.
[SIGHS] In the same cellar is a brother and a sister, hide there.
She [INAUDIBLE] for him.
Now when the brother and sister [? hears, ?] I gonna take you out-- [? it's true-- ?] the brother and sister-- Hela help us too.
It's a big cellar, a lot of potatoes there.
She eat the potatoes raw.
Raw potatoes.
And take her out.
[INAUDIBLE] she said, listen, here's a brother and a sister.
Help him take.
It's [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what it mean?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Not a good word, no.
To German, it look like this.
And I'll take that.
I'll take the two girls in the [INAUDIBLE].
And the boy in the back, and the police from [?
Latvia, ?] [?
Latvian ?] police, watched there.
Shoots to him.
Now, shoots back.
He's drunk.
The officer is drunk.
[INAUDIBLE].
The brother and the sister have clothes on them.
Now, my friend, she didn't have nothing.
She had on a gown-- nightgown.
I take that to the Polish family that give me shoes, stocking, a dress, and a-- and a coat.
She get this.
They said, you free.
She ask them, why you doing this?
You're German, and I'm Jewish.
He said, I believe in God.
And I'm a religion man.
And I want do the something for God.
I saved her life and one person.
I killed a lot of people.
That is some story.
Wait a minute.
This is not all.
She [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she went-- she is in the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she is in Kovel.
And she meet her sister.
No.
This was before-- from there, from the ghetto, she left to look her sister in Kovel, [INAUDIBLE].
And she find her sister.
And she find her sister in the same town the Germans take every Jew from one place to take to Auschwitz.
And there's a Jewish policeman.
Not that have no guns, and a guard, you know?
And she tell him, listen, I have Polish papers.
Help me, because I'm Jew.
Said, you see, here's my mother.
These are my children.
This is my mom-- mine wife.
I now can help you.
Because I got-- the German got to me too.
In the meantime, comes over a German officer and, what she wanted?
I don't know, [? officer, ?] she said she is lost.
She's Catholic.
Show the papers.
Listen, one time more, you come to the Jew, I could kill you.
Out from here!
She left.
[INAUDIBLE].
And she-- somebody told her in Russian, a Polish man, Ukraine.
Stop.
I went to her.
I said, I know you're Jewish.
I know you work in the-- in the flea markets or somewhere else.
Said, I'm not going to kill you.
Come with me here, in this house.
I have a cape.
Under the cape was a lot of silk.
Because I taked from stores.
I stole it from stores.
And said, I could take off my everything, the shoes and everything.
You bend around these silk.
Then I save your life.
You can go.
And she do this.
Said, I take you to my father and mother.
She said, no.
And I found my friend with a horse, with a wagon.
She jumped up there and she left.
And she come back to Lublin.
And she come back to Lublin, we had to go.
She have a friend, a boy, who work by the train.
Like a physical worker, I take [? out ?] the [INAUDIBLE], you know?
She find him.
She find him.
And she talked with him.
In the meantime, the same time, the German SS-- you know the SS--
--come with a special truck, where they're putting people in the truck, and put [INAUDIBLE] [? killed them ?] in the truck.
And take her too.
And she screamed, I'm not Jewish!
And the men what they work, they are Polish men, with the work by the trains, says what did-- I tell don't take me.
I'm not Jewish.
I went to them officers.
I said, she is not Jewish.
She is Catholic.
Why you take her?
Get out of here.
What she have to do?
She went to Berlin.
She-- I went and told them that she once worked for the German.
She was Jewish.
Whoa.
And she worked by farmer.
And there's a lot of other--
OK, I'll finish.
Camp.
She-- the name is Sarah Fishman. 1954, she bore my son.
Wroclaw.
After 15 days, she died.
She had complications.
She had-- at this time, you don't have no penicillin, not nothing.
She died.
The whole family in Lublin.
I went to Warsaw, with my older sister.
When I come there, my heart start to beat so hard.
Because she lived in this building.
Somebody lived there.
I knocked the door.
An older couple, a Polish couple, had opened the door.
I told them hello, good morning.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can go, you know.
Come in.
OK, come in.
Very nice people.
I tell him listen, [? before ?] my sister lived here, with her husband, with five children, five sons.
Five sons.
You wanna hear?
Nobody comes here?
No, no, no.
You're the first when you come here.
I went to the-- where they cleaned the-- the old building, and full buildings, high buildings, high for Warsaw.
I recognized the daughter her-- her daughter.
She said, I remember you.
You come for your sister here, yeah?
With my young boy, I stayed with my sister some there.
And I went, where's your mother?
She said, she has a cold.
I go in.
When I come in, I told her hello.
You-- you remember me?
No.
And I see there, furniture from my sister.
I see the bed which sometimes I sleep there, in this bed.
I told her, listen, I don't want nothing from you.
Tell me what happened.
Nobody lived with this name here.
And I walk out.
She's afraid something.
I don't know, whatever.
And she's sick.
She's old.
You know?
But you ultimately did find out what happened to your family?
I've told you, from Hitler, she [INAUDIBLE] in 1943.
And my older sister, she in the ghetto, they killed her in the ghetto.
[INAUDIBLE] the children.
Nobody.
No cousins, no nothing.
I remember my mother's name is Zemel.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She have the brother [LAUGHS] what lived here.
No, I no have the address.
I no have nothing.
Because the house had burned up.
And I write a letter to Warsaw, to the HIAS.
And I told them, I have uncle.
The name is [?
Josep ?] Zemel.
Lived in Philadelphia.
[LAUGHS] And that's all.
You know what?
In two weeks, [INTERPOSING VOICES] I get the address for Warsaw.
And the telephone, and the name, everything.
I wrote a letter in Jewish [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to mine uncle.
No answer.
I wrote him.
The next-- no, in the meantime, I get a letter from Philadelphia, from the HIAS.
As one worker from HIAS-- HIAS went to your uncle.
He talked with him.
And oh yeah, then I get a letter from mine uncle.
I said you, this is true, everything.
I'd gone out to Florida.
He want-- when I come back, I take you.
I'll bring you over to America.
This is in 1948.
And I get this letter, and I get a package.
Care, ?] [? label-- ?] this time, they suspended packages a little.
[INAUDIBLE].
And I get no more letters.
I had sent him another letter, don't answer me.
I no like [INAUDIBLE].
No, I have-- in 1963--
In January, I don't know-- [LAUGHS] I don't know if I'll open this.
It was still a dream with mine uncle, I never saw in my life.
I sit down, and I write a letter to mine uncle, after 15 years.
After three weeks, I get a letter.
Not from mine uncle, but from my cousins, from the older daughter.
And she wrote to me in English.
I called for somebody.
Not that many people know English in Poland at this time.
And then she wrote me a letter.
She can write in Polish.
But the neighbor is from Poland.
What?
She asked me a lot of questions, like you.
She sent me, in 1963, right away, an invitation to come for a visit.
I come over.
Myself.
A visit.
I come over for a visit.
Very hard to go from Poland, by the Communist [INAUDIBLE].
Oh, to [INAUDIBLE] like this, I [INAUDIBLE] somebody.
I come over.
I stay here two months.
I bring papers, a diploma from school.
[CRYING] And I went to the HIAS.
And this time, the name from the HIAS, [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE], I remember her name, so many years.
[INAUDIBLE].
She very nice.
And I keep every papers from my cousins and make copies.
I have other papers.
I am a champion [INAUDIBLE].
You know?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I said, I want to stay here.
I never want to go back.
Said, no.
The HIAS said, you go back in two months.
You come over.
The American government gets you the visa.
I went back to Poland.
This is true.
In a month, the American Embassy of Warsaw sent me a letter to come over.
I come over.
And what I want tell you--
